exercises.prompt: You have two ropes, each of which takes exactly 60 minutes to burn from end to end — but each burns unevenly (faster at some points along its length, slower at others), and you have no clock. Using only these two ropes and a lighter, how can you measure exactly 45 minutes? Work out a method, then reveal and compare.
exercises.answer: Light rope 1 at both ends and rope 2 at one end, all at the same moment. Rope 1 finishes burning after exactly 30 minutes (lighting both ends always halves the burn time, regardless of unevenness). The instant rope 1 finishes, light the remaining end of rope 2 as well; its remaining length — whatever fraction that is — now burns from both ends and finishes in half the time it would have taken from one end, which is exactly 15 more minutes. 30 + 15 = 45 minutes total.
exercises.explanation: The key insight is that lighting a rope from both ends always halves its total burn time to 30 minutes, no matter how unevenly it burns, because the two flames together consume the same 60-minute's worth of rope.

exercises.prompt: All zorks are quibs. No quib is a dax. Which conclusion necessarily follows?
exercises.explanation: This is the valid syllogism form Celarent: All Z are Q, No Q is D, therefore No Z is D.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following is logically equivalent to $\neg(P \lor Q)$?
exercises.explanation: De Morgan's law: the negation of an 'or' is the 'and' of the negations.

exercises.prompt: A rare disease affects 1% of the population. A test for it is 95% accurate in both directions (95% of sick people test positive, and 95% of healthy people test negative). If a random person tests positive, what is the probability, to the nearest whole percent, that they actually have the disease?
exercises.explanation: Out of 10,000 people: 100 are sick, of whom 95 test positive; 9,900 are healthy, of whom 5% (495) false-positive. Of the 590 total positives, only 95 are truly sick: $95/590 \approx 16\%$. Because the disease is rare, false positives from the huge healthy population swamp the true positives — the classic base-rate fallacy.

exercises.prompt: You start facing north and walk 3 blocks. You turn right and walk 2 blocks. You turn right again and walk 3 blocks. You turn left and walk 2 blocks. Which best describes where you end up?
exercises.explanation: Track it as coordinates: north 3 → (0,3); turn right (now facing east), east 2 → (2,3); turn right (facing south), south 3 → (2,0); turn left (facing east), east 2 → (4,0). You end 4 blocks from the origin, facing east.

exercises.prompt: Two players alternate removing 1, 2, or 3 stones from a pile of 17. Whoever removes the last stone wins. You move first. How many stones should you take on your first move to guarantee a win with optimal play afterward?
exercises.explanation: The losing positions (for the player about to move) are multiples of 4, since whatever 1-3 stones your opponent takes, you can always take enough to land back on a multiple of 4. $17 = 4\times 4 + 1$, so taking 1 stone leaves 16 — a multiple of 4 — for your opponent.
